learning_outcomes.description: Collaborate with a partner or group to produce spoken and written work in Japanese.
Reflect on and respond to feedback on your translation and interpreting work, and use this to improve.
Demonstrate basic skills in Japanese/English interpreting and translation.
Understand the role of interpreter and translator in the society.
Demonstrate knowledge and respect for ethics and ethical standards of professional practitioners.
Critically analyse texts within their sociohistorical contexts.

assessments.due_date:
9/11/2026 1:00 pm

Monday, EXAM WEEK 1

This Learner Diary is based on participation from Weeks 1 to13 of the course, but is submitted in exam block, after classes have finished.
assessments.category: Notebook/ Logbook, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
This assessment encourages student to actively take up the course goal of improving their Japanese proficiency through interpreting and translation, by reflecting on their learning and developing their own self-study resources. The Diary is based on participation in Weeks 1-13 of the course, but is submitted in exam block, after classes have finished.

Submission includes:

Diary Entries (20 entries x 1 mark each = 20 marks):

For each session you participate in, complete a diary entry using the template provided on blackboard. Make notes during class or straight after about any new grammar and vocabulary you encounter, and other learning experiences such as interpreting and translation challenges, and different approaches and solutions.

Each entry is marked for the student’s participation in the session, as reflected in their submission; therefore, students may only submit diary entries for sessions they participated in. Other entries will not be marked.

Note that a public translation workshop will be announced at the beginning of the semester, and participation in this session is required. Students who are unable to attend this event must contact the Course Coordinator as soon as possible to make alternative arrangements for this assessment.

Self-Study Resource & Learner Reflection (10 marks):

Collate all of the new vocabulary and grammar you noted throughout the course into a self-study resource. For at least 5 new grammar points, write explanations and example sentences to show your grasp. Cite reputable Japanese dictionaries, textbooks, and learning resources. Reference your citations using MLA referencing style. On your self-study resource, write a sentence about why you have chosen to use this layout and order.

Write a reflection (300-400 words) about what you enjoyed and found challenging in this course, articulating what you have learned and how this will affect your future study or work related to Japan and Japanese language. Write a flowing piece rather than dot points. 

Refer to full guidelines and marking rubric on Blackboard.

AI and MT Statement

Note: While Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are useful tools for professionals and are addressed in this course, the focus is developing students' Japanese proficiency through translation and interpreting activities.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies in preparation, it is not recommended as it will not adequately prepare them for the in-person component of this assessment. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

The use of generative AI or MT tools in the in-person component of this assessment will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Upload a single word document or PDF file of your Learner Diary on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Role play/ Simulation
assessments.task_description:
Transcribe the first (or from the specified starting point) 800 characters each of the assigned two SBS Radio programs in Japanese. English speech (if any) is not included in the transcription. Use kanji as much as possible. Include annotations about your transcription challenges and choices. 

Duration of task: It depends on the student's familiarity with Japanese radio programs, but it is expected to take about three hours each to complete the task.

See Blackboard for the link to the programs, guidelines for annotations, marking criteria, and other details.

AI and MT Statement

Note: While Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are useful tools for professionals and are addressed in this course, the focus is developing students' Japanese proficiency through translation and interpreting activities.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.due_date:
4/09/2026 1:00 pm

Friday, WEEK 6

The due date provided for this assessment is for submitting the completed dialogue with annotations, which is based on the in-class activity. Students who are unable to attend the class session should apply for an extension and contact the course coordinator to organise to complete the role-play during another week.
assessments.task_description:
All students participate in the role-play interpreting practices in the T02 (Wednesday) session in WEEK 5 and WEEK 6. After the role-play interpreting practices, students fill the interpreter’s sections of the dialogues. Use the template. Write at least five annotations (comments/explanations) each for your translation (in English) in the right column. 

The due date provided for this assessment is for submitting the completed dialogue with annotations, which is based on the in-class activity. Students who are unable to attend the class session should apply for an extension and contact the course coordinator to organise to complete the role-play during another week.

Annotation examples:

  • The original word is 親戚の浅野さん. As no information available about gender, I assumed it is a woman and used “she” here.
  • The original utterance was quite long. I decided to break it into two parts.

assessments.due_date:
20/10/2026 - 28/10/2026

Students are assigned a presentation slot in T2 (Wednesday) in either WEEK 12 or WEEK 13.

The annotated translation is due at 1pm on Tuesday, the day before assigned day of your presentation, either WEEK 12 or WEEK 13.
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Presentation, Translation/ Interpretation
assessments.task_description:
Select a text to translate from the options provided on Blackboard in WEEK 10 (for students presenting in Week 12) and WEEK 11 (for students presenting in Week 13).

Translate the text and annotate the translation, including: new vocabulary and grammar acquired, translation challenges, and solutions proposed.

*Students submit their written work by 1pm on the day before their presentation.

Give a presentation (3-5 minutes), in Week 12 or Week 13 tutorial as assigned:

  • summarise the annotated translation (ie key translation challenges and solutions)
  • compare your experience of group translation (used in class sessions and workshop) to individual translation (used for this task)

Presentations will be followed by Q&A and class discussion on the points raised.

Students are expected to actively participate in the Q&A sessions of their peers' presentations, and reflect on the presentation content in their learner diaries.
